title: 21 Hidar — The Entry of the Virgin into the Temple & The Assembly
narrative:
Hidar

The Entry of the Holy Virgin Mary into the Temple

The Holy Virgin Mary, Theotokos (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Gregory of Nyssa, Bishop and Theologian

The twenty-first day of Hidar is given to one of the deepest and most luminous feasts of the entire Ethiopian liturgical year: the Entry of the Holy Virgin Mary into the Temple. She was three years old, by the tradition's account, when Joachim and Anne brought her to the Temple precincts in Jerusalem and when she walked alone — without hesitation, without turning back, without the need of a guiding hand — up the fifteen steps into the sanctuary. The High Priest Zechariah received her and led her, against all custom and against every rule of the Temple's restricted spaces, into the Holy of Holies itself — the innermost chamber, the place of the divine Name, the space behind the veil where no one entered except the High Priest on the Day of Atonement, and he not without blood. Into this space, this child walked. Into this space, she settled. And what the Temple theology of the Tewahedo tradition sees in this moment is not primarily the extraordinary privilege conferred on an exceptional child but the theological disclosure of who she already was: the child who walked into the Holy of Holies without fear was the one whose nature had always been the Holy of Holies — the sanctuary prepared from before time for the indwelling of the Most High.

The theology of Mary as the true Holy of Holies is not a metaphor in the Tewahedo tradition — it is the precise doctrinal statement about the Incarnation's architecture. The Holy of Holies in the Jerusalem Temple was the space where the Presence of God dwelt above the Mercy Seat, between the Cherubim, in the cloud that filled the sanctuary. The Ark of the Covenant was the vessel that contained the signs of the Covenant: the manna, the rod, the tablets. Mary is the Ark of the New Covenant: she contained not the signs of the Covenant but the Covenant Himself — not the manna but the Bread of Life, not the rod but the High Priest who is also the sacrifice, not the tablets of the Law but the incarnate Word who is the Law's fulfillment. When the tradition names her the Holy of Holies, it is saying that the Temple's most sacred space was always a prophecy of her, that the veil that concealed the divine Presence from human eyes was always pointing to the veil of her flesh through which the divine Presence would finally, once and permanently, be disclosed.

The Entry of Mary into the Temple is simultaneously the Entry of the human soul into the divine Presence — and this is why the Tewahedo tradition honors it with a depth that resonates through the entire apostolic month of Hidar. The apostolic vesting that Hidar has been distributing is not ultimately a commission for external activity but a commission for interior transformation: the soul is vested by being admitted to the divine Presence, by walking into the Holy of Holies of the divine intimacy, by taking up residence in the space where the Mercy Seat is. Mary's three-year-old entry is the template for every soul's entry — the fearless, unturning, unhesitating movement into the innermost place, the willingness to dwell where the divine fire burns. The high priest who receives her at the top of the fifteen steps is the image of the divine welcome extended to every soul that approaches without turning back.

St. Gregory of Nyssa, the fourth-century Bishop of Nyssa and younger brother of Basil the Great, provides the day's assembly with its theological voice in the fullest patristic sense. His Life of Moses — which reads Moses's ascent of Sinai as a map of the soul's ascent into the divine darkness — is the patristic text most naturally suited to the Entry's theology: the movement from the outer courts to the Holy of Holies is Gregory's mountain of the soul's ascent, and the darkness in which Moses encounters God is the same darkness that Dionysius the Areopagite later calls the light too bright for ordinary sight. Gregory in the assembly of the Entry is the theologian who explains what the three-year-old is doing — who gives the community the vocabulary for the experience that Mary embodies, the language for the inner journey that every baptized soul is commissioned to make. The apostolic vesting of Hidar, which began with the outward commission, has arrived on its twenty-first day at the inward commission — and the two are one.
